error.signature: SSRF attack accesses cloud metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254) via user-supplied URL

verdict.summary: Cloud instances (AWS/GCP/Azure) have a metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254 that returns IAM credentials, API keys, and instance configuration. If your app fetches user-supplied URLs, attackers can read cloud credentials via SSRF.

dead_ends.why_fails: Attackers bypass with: decimal IP (2852039166), IPv6 (::ffff:169.254.169.254), DNS rebinding, redirects from allowed domains, or alternate representations.

dead_ends.action: Use URL parsing to extract hostname and block known-bad IPs
dead_ends.why_fails: URL parsers are inconsistent. http://169.254.169.254@evil.com, http://[::ffff:a9fe:a9fe], and http://0xa9fea9fe all resolve to the metadata endpoint.

workarounds.action: Require IMDSv2 (token-based) on all cloud instances
workarounds.how: aws ec2 modify-instance-metadata-options --instance-id i-xxx --http-tokens required  # blocks simple SSRF

workarounds.action: Resolve DNS and validate the IP address is not private/link-local before fetching
workarounds.how: import ipaddress; ip = socket.getaddrinfo(hostname, port)[0][4][0]; if ipaddress.ip_address(ip).is_private: reject()

workarounds.action: Use network-level controls: block metadata endpoint access from application subnets
workarounds.how: iptables -A OUTPUT -d 169.254.169.254 -j DROP  # or use VPC network policies to block metadata access
